**Eastern Christianity** comprises [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") traditions and [church families](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination") that originally developed during [classical](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity") and [late antiquity](/wiki/Late_antiquity "Late antiquity") in the [Eastern Mediterranean](/wiki/Eastern_Mediterranean "Eastern Mediterranean") region or locations further east, south or north.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The term does not describe a single [communion](/wiki/Communion_(Christian) "Communion (Christian)") or [religious denomination](/wiki/Religious_denomination "Religious denomination"). Eastern Christianity is a category distinguished from [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity"), which is composed of those Christian traditions and churches that originally developed further west.

Major Eastern Christian bodies include the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") and the [Oriental Orthodox Churches](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox_Churches "Oriental Orthodox Churches"), along with those groups descended from the historic [Church of the East](/wiki/Church_of_the_East "Church of the East") (also called the [Assyrian Church](/wiki/Assyrian_Church_(disambiguation) "Assyrian Church (disambiguation)")), as well as the [Eastern Catholic Churches](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches") (which are in communion with [Rome](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") while maintaining [Eastern liturgies](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_liturgy "Eastern Catholic liturgy")), and the [Eastern Protestant churches](/wiki/Eastern_Protestant_Christianity "Eastern Protestant Christianity").[[2]](#cite_note-HämmerliMayer2016-2) Most Eastern churches do not normally refer to themselves as "Eastern", with the exception of the [Assyrian Church of the East](/wiki/Assyrian_Church_of_the_East "Assyrian Church of the East") and its offshoot, the [Ancient Church of the East](/wiki/Ancient_Church_of_the_East "Ancient Church of the East").

The [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy") are the largest body within Eastern Christianity with a worldwide population of 220 million,[[3]](#cite_note-about-3) followed by the [Oriental Orthodox](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox_Churches "Oriental Orthodox Churches") at 60 million.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The [Eastern Catholic Churches](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches") consist of about 16–18 million and are a small minority within the Catholic Church.[[5]](#cite_note-5) [Eastern Protestant Christian](/wiki/Eastern_Protestant_Christianity "Eastern Protestant Christianity") churches do not form a single communion; churches like the [Ukrainian Lutheran Church](/wiki/Ukrainian_Lutheran_Church "Ukrainian Lutheran Church") and the [Malankara Mar Thoma Syrian Church](/wiki/Malankara_Mar_Thoma_Syrian_Church "Malankara Mar Thoma Syrian Church") have under a million members. The Assyrian Church of the East and the Ancient Church of the East, descendant churches of the Assyria-based Church of the East, have a combined membership of approximately 400,000.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Historically, Eastern Christianity was centered in the Middle East and surrounding areas, where Christianity originated. However, after the [Muslim conquest of the Levant](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_the_Levant "Muslim conquest of the Levant") in the 7th century, the term **Eastern Church** increasingly came to be used for the [Greek Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") centered in [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople"), in contrast with the (*Western*) [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church"), centered on [Rome](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See"), which uses the [Latin liturgical rites](/wiki/Latin_liturgical_rites "Latin liturgical rites"). The terms "Eastern" and "Western" in this regard originated with geographical divisions in Christianity mirroring the cultural divide between the [Hellenistic East and the Latin West](/wiki/Greek_East_and_Latin_West "Greek East and Latin West"), and the political divide of 395 AD between the [Western](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire") and [Eastern Roman Empires](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"). Since the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") of the 16th century, the term "Eastern Christianity" may be used in contrast with "[Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity")", which contains not only the Latin Church but also forms of [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") and [Independent Catholicism](/wiki/Independent_Catholicism "Independent Catholicism").[[7]](#cite_note-CSSR1976-7) Some Eastern churches have more in common historically and theologically with Western Christianity than with one another.

Because the largest church in the East is the body currently known as the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), the term "Orthodox" is often used in a similar fashion to "Eastern", to refer to specific historical Christian communions. However, strictly speaking, most Christian denominations, whether Eastern or Western, regard themselves as "[orthodox](/wiki/Orthodoxy "Orthodoxy")" (meaning "following correct beliefs") as well as "[catholic](/wiki/Catholicity "Catholicity")" (meaning "universal"), and as sharing in the [Four Marks of the Church](/wiki/Four_Marks_of_the_Church "Four Marks of the Church") listed in the [Nicene-Constantinopolitan Creed](/wiki/Nicene-Constantinopolitan_Creed "Nicene-Constantinopolitan Creed") (381 AD): "One, Holy, Catholic and Apostolic" ([Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"): μία, ἁγία, καθολικὴ καὶ ἀποστολικὴ ἐκκλησία).[[note 1]](#cite_note-9)

Eastern churches (excepting the non-liturgical dissenting bodies) utilize several [liturgical rites](/wiki/Christian_liturgy "Christian liturgy"): the [Alexandrian Rite](/wiki/Alexandrian_Rite "Alexandrian Rite"), the [Armenian Rite](/wiki/Armenian_Rite "Armenian Rite"), the [Byzantine Rite](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite "Byzantine Rite"), the [East Syriac Rite](/wiki/East_Syriac_Rite "East Syriac Rite") (also known as Persian or Assyrian Rite), and the [West Syriac Rite](/wiki/West_Syriac_Rite "West Syriac Rite") (also called the Antiochian Rite).

Eastern Christians do not all share the same religious traditions, but many do share cultural traditions. Christianity divided itself in the East during its early centuries both within and outside of the Roman Empire in disputes about [Christology](/wiki/Christology "Christology") and fundamental theology, as well as through national divisions (Roman, Persian, etc.). It would be many centuries later that Western Christianity fully split from these traditions as its own communion. Major branches or families of Eastern Christianity, each holding a distinct [theology](/wiki/Theology "Theology") and [dogma](/wiki/Dogma "Dogma"), include the Eastern Orthodox Church, the Oriental Orthodox communion, the Eastern Catholic Churches and the Assyrian Church of the East.[[9]](#cite_note-HindsonMitchell2013-10)

In most Eastern churches, parish priests administer the sacrament of [chrismation](/wiki/Chrismation "Chrismation") to infants after [baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism"), and priests are allowed to marry before ordination. The Eastern Catholic Churches recognize the authority of the [Pope of Rome](/wiki/Pope "Pope"), but some of them who have originally been part of the Orthodox Church or Oriental Orthodox churches closely follow the traditions of Orthodoxy or Oriental Orthodoxy, including the tradition of allowing married men to become priests.

The Eastern churches' differences from Western Christianity have to do with [theology](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_%E2%80%93_Roman_Catholic_theological_differences "Eastern Orthodox – Roman Catholic theological differences"), as well as [liturgy](/wiki/Liturgy "Liturgy"), culture, language, and politics. For the non-Catholic Eastern churches, a definitive date for the commencement of [schism](/wiki/Schism_(religion) "Schism (religion)") cannot usually be given (see [East–West Schism](/wiki/East%E2%80%93West_Schism "East–West Schism")). The Church of the East declared independence from the churches of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") at its general council in 424, which was before the [Council of Ephesus](/wiki/Council_of_Ephesus "Council of Ephesus") in 431, and so had nothing to do with the theology declared at that council. Oriental Orthodoxy separated after the [Council of Chalcedon](/wiki/Council_of_Chalcedon "Council of Chalcedon") in 451 but did not immediately form separate patriarchates until 518 (in the case of the Syriac Patriarchate of Antioch) and 536 (in the case of the Coptic Patriarchate of Alexandria).

Since the time of the historian [Edward Gibbon](/wiki/Edward_Gibbon "Edward Gibbon"), the split between the Church of Rome and the Orthodox Church has been conveniently dated to 1054, though the reality is more complex. This split is sometimes referred to as the *Great Schism*, but is now more usually called the [East–West Schism](/wiki/East%E2%80%93West_Schism "East–West Schism"). This final schism reflected a larger cultural and political division which had developed in Europe and Southwest Asia during the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") and coincided with Western Europe's re-emergence from the [collapse of the Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Fall of the Western Roman Empire").

The Ukrainian Lutheran Church developed within [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Eastern_Europe) "Galicia (Eastern Europe)") around 1926, with its rites being based on the [Liturgy of Saint John Chrysostom](/wiki/Liturgy_of_Saint_John_Chrysostom "Liturgy of Saint John Chrysostom"), rather than on the Western *[Formula Missae](/wiki/Formula_missae "Formula missae")*.[[10]](#cite_note-Bebis2013-11)[[11]](#cite_note-Webber1992-12)

### Eastern Orthodox Church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Christianity&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Eastern Orthodox Church")]

Further information: [History of the Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/History_of_the_Eastern_Orthodox_Church "History of the Eastern Orthodox Church")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Christ_Hagia_Sofia.jpg/250px-Christ_Hagia_Sofia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christ_Hagia_Sofia.jpg)

Christ Pantocrator, detail of the Deesis mosaic in [Hagia Sophia](/wiki/Hagia_Sophia "Hagia Sophia") – [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") ([Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul")) 12th century

The Eastern Orthodox Church is a Christian body whose adherents are largely based in Western Asia (particularly [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria"), [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon"), [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan"), [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"), and [Palestine](/wiki/Palestine "Palestine")) and [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"), [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe"), the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans") and the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus") ([Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(country) "Georgia (country)")), with a growing presence in the [Western world](/wiki/Western_world "Western world"). Eastern Orthodox Christians accept the decisions of the [first seven ecumenical councils](/wiki/First_seven_Ecumenical_Councils "First seven Ecumenical Councils").

Eastern Orthodox Christianity identifies itself as the original Christian church (see [early centers of Christianity](/wiki/Early_centers_of_Christianity "Early centers of Christianity")) founded by Christ and the Apostles, and traces its lineage back to the [early Church](/wiki/Early_church "Early church") through the process of [apostolic succession](/wiki/Apostolic_succession "Apostolic succession") and unchanged theology and practice. Characteristics of the Eastern Orthodox Church include the [Byzantine Rite](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite "Byzantine Rite") (shared with some Eastern Catholic Churches) and an emphasis on the continuation of [Holy Tradition](/wiki/Sacred_tradition "Sacred tradition"), which it holds to be apostolic in nature.

The Eastern Orthodox Church is organized into self-governing jurisdictions along geographical, national, ethnic or linguistic lines. Eastern Orthodoxy is thus made up of fourteen or sixteen [autocephalous](/wiki/Autocephaly "Autocephaly") bodies. Smaller churches are autonomous and each have a mother church that is autocephalous.

All Eastern Orthodox are united in doctrinal agreement with each other, though a few are not in communion at present, for non-doctrinal reasons. This is in contrast to the Catholic Church and its various churches. Members of the latter are all in communion with each other, parts of a top-down [hierarchy](/wiki/Hierarchy "Hierarchy") (see *[primus inter pares](/wiki/Primus_inter_pares "Primus inter pares")*). The Eastern Orthodox reject the [Filioque clause](/wiki/Filioque "Filioque") as contrast to Catholics. The Catholic Church was once in communion with the Eastern Orthodox Church, but the two split after the [East–West Schism](/wiki/East%E2%80%93West_Schism "East–West Schism") and are no longer in communion.

It is estimated that there are approximately 240 million Eastern Orthodox Christians in the world.[[note 2]](#cite_note-13) Today, many adherents shun the term "Eastern" as denying the church's universal character. They refer to Eastern Orthodoxy simply as the *Orthodox Church*.[[12]](#cite_note-14)

### Oriental Orthodoxy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Christianity&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Oriental Orthodoxy")]

Main article: [Oriental Orthodox Churches](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox_Churches "Oriental Orthodox Churches")

Oriental Orthodoxy refers to the churches of Eastern Christian tradition that keep the faith of the first three [ecumenical councils](/wiki/Ecumenical_Council "Ecumenical Council") of the [undivided Christian Church](/wiki/State_church_of_the_Roman_Empire "State church of the Roman Empire"): the [First Council of Nicaea](/wiki/First_Council_of_Nicaea "First Council of Nicaea") (AD 325), the [First Council of Constantinople](/wiki/First_Council_of_Constantinople "First Council of Constantinople") (381) and the [Council of Ephesus](/wiki/Council_of_Ephesus "Council of Ephesus") (431), while rejecting the [dogmatic definitions](/wiki/Dogmatic_definition "Dogmatic definition") of the [Council of Chalcedon](/wiki/Council_of_Chalcedon "Council of Chalcedon") (451). Hence, these churches are also called the *Old Oriental churches*. They comprise the [Coptic Orthodox Church](/wiki/Coptic_Orthodox_Church "Coptic Orthodox Church"), the [Malankara Orthodox Church](/wiki/Malankara_Orthodox_Church "Malankara Orthodox Church") (India), the [Eritrean Orthodox Tewahedo Church](/wiki/Eritrean_Orthodox_Tewahedo_Church "Eritrean Orthodox Tewahedo Church"), the [Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church](/wiki/Ethiopian_Orthodox_Tewahedo_Church "Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church"), the [Syriac Orthodox Church](/wiki/Syriac_Orthodox_Church "Syriac Orthodox Church") and the [Armenian Apostolic Church](/wiki/Armenian_Apostolic_Church "Armenian Apostolic Church").

Oriental Orthodoxy developed in reaction to Chalcedon on the eastern limit of the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") and in [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), Syria and [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"). In those locations, there are also Eastern Orthodox [patriarchs](/wiki/Patriarch "Patriarch"), but the rivalry between the two has largely vanished in the centuries since the schism.

### Church of the East

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Christianity&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Church of the East")]

Main articles: [Church of the East](/wiki/Church_of_the_East "Church of the East"), [Nestorianism](/wiki/Nestorianism "Nestorianism"), and [Lakhmids](/wiki/Lakhmids "Lakhmids")

Historically, the Church of the East was the widest reaching branch of Eastern Christianity, at its height spreading from its heartland in [Persian](/wiki/Persia "Persia")-ruled [Assyria](/wiki/Assyria "Assyria") to the Mediterranean, India, and China. Originally the only Christian church recognized by [Zoroastrian](/wiki/Zoroastrian "Zoroastrian")-led [Sassanid Persia](/wiki/Sassanid_Empire "Sassanid Empire") (through its alliance with the Lakhmids, the regional rivals to the Byzantines and its [Ghassanid](/wiki/Ghassanid "Ghassanid") vassal), the Church of the East declared itself independent of other churches in 424 and over the next century became affiliated with Nestorianism, a Christological doctrine advanced by [Nestorius](/wiki/Nestorius "Nestorius"), [Patriarch of Constantinople](/wiki/Ecumenical_Patriarch_of_Constantinople "Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople") from 428 to 431, which had been declared heretical in the Roman Empire. Thereafter it was often known in the West, possibly inaccurately, as the Nestorian Church. Surviving a period of persecution within Persia, the Church of the East flourished under the [Abbasid Caliphate](/wiki/Abbasid_Caliphate "Abbasid Caliphate") and branched out, establishing dioceses throughout Asia. After another period of expansion under the [Mongol Empire](/wiki/Mongol_Empire "Mongol Empire"), the church went into decline starting in the 14th century, and was eventually largely confined to its founding [Assyrian](/wiki/Assyrian_people "Assyrian people") adherent's heartland in the [Assyrian homeland](/wiki/Assyrian_homeland "Assyrian homeland"), although another remnant survived on the [Malabar Coast](/wiki/Malabar_Coast "Malabar Coast") of India.

In the 16th century, dynastic struggles sent the church into schism, resulting in the formation of two rival churches: The [Chaldean Catholic Church](/wiki/Chaldean_Catholic_Church "Chaldean Catholic Church"), which entered into communion with Rome as an Eastern Catholic Church, and the Assyrian Church of the East. The followers of these two churches are almost exclusively ethnic Assyrians. In India, the local Church of the East community, known as the Saint Thomas Christians, experienced its own rifts as a result of Portuguese influence.

#### Assyrian Church of the East

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Christianity&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Assyrian Church of the East")]

Main articles: [Church of the East](/wiki/Church_of_the_East "Church of the East") and [Assyrian Church of the East](/wiki/Assyrian_Church_of_the_East "Assyrian Church of the East")

The Assyrian Church of the East emerged from the historical Church of the East, which was centered in Mesopotamia/Assyria, then part of the [Persian Empire](/wiki/Sasanian_Empire "Sasanian Empire"), and spread widely throughout Asia. The modern Assyrian Church of the East emerged in the 16th century following a split with the Chaldean Church, which later entered into communion with Rome as an Eastern Catholic Church.

The Church of the East was associated with the doctrine of Nestorianism, advanced by Nestorius, Patriarch of Constantinople from 428 to 431, which emphasized the disunion between the human and divine natures of [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus"). Nestorius and his doctrine were condemned at the Council of Ephesus in 431, leading to the [Nestorian Schism](/wiki/Nestorian_Schism "Nestorian Schism") in which churches supporting Nestorius split from the rest of Christianity.

Many followers relocated to Persia and became affiliated with the local Christian community there. This community adopted an increasingly Nestorian theology and was thereafter often known as the Nestorian Church. As such, the Church of the East accepts only the first two ecumenical councils of the undivided Church—the First Council of Nicaea and the First Council of Constantinople—as defining its faith tradition, and rapidly took a different course from other Eastern Christians.

The Church of the East spread widely through Persia and into Asia, being introduced to India by the 6th century and to the Mongols and China in the 7th century. It experienced periodic expansion until the 14th century, when the church was nearly destroyed by the collapse of the Mongol Empire and the conquests of [Timur](/wiki/Timur "Timur"). By the 16th century it was largely confined to Iraq, northeast Syria, southeast Turkey, northwest [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") and the Malabar Coast of India ([Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala")). The split of the 15th century, which saw the emergence of separate Assyrian and Chaldean Churches, left only the former as an independent sect. Additional splits into the 20th century further affected the history of the Assyrian Church of the East.

#### Saint Thomas Syrian Christians

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Christianity&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Saint Thomas Syrian Christians")]

Main article: [Saint Thomas Christians](/wiki/Saint_Thomas_Christians "Saint Thomas Christians")

The [Saint Thomas Syrian Christians](/wiki/Saint_Thomas_Christians "Saint Thomas Christians") are an ancient body of [Syrian Christians](/wiki/Syriac_Christianity "Syriac Christianity") in Kerala, Malabar coast of [India](/wiki/India "India") who trace their origins to the evangelical activity of [Thomas the Apostle](/wiki/Thomas_the_Apostle "Thomas the Apostle") in the 1st century. Many [Assyrian](/wiki/Assyrian_people "Assyrian people") and [Jewish](/wiki/Jews "Jews") communities like the [Knanaya](/wiki/Knanaya "Knanaya") and the [Cochin Jews](/wiki/Cochin_Jews "Cochin Jews") assimilated into the [Saint Thomas Syrian Christian](/wiki/Saint_Thomas_Christian "Saint Thomas Christian") community.[[13]](#cite_note-AFM-15) By the 5th century the Saint Thomas Syrian Christians were part of the [Church of the East](/wiki/Church_of_the_East "Church of the East") ([Nestorian Church](/wiki/Nestorian_Church "Nestorian Church")). Until the middle of the 17th century and the arrival of the [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_India "Portuguese India"), the Thomas Christians were all one in faith and rite. Thereafter, divisions arose among them, and consequently they are today of several different rites. The [East Syriac Chaldean Rite](/wiki/East_Syriac_Rite "East Syriac Rite") (Edessan Rite) Churches among the Saint Thomas Syrian Christians are the [Syro Malabar Church](/wiki/Syro-Malabar_Church "Syro-Malabar Church") and the [Chaldean Syrian Church](/wiki/Chaldean_Syrian_Church "Chaldean Syrian Church"). The [West Syriac Antiochian Rite](/wiki/West_Syriac_Rite "West Syriac Rite") Churches among the Saint Thomas Syrian Christians are the [Malankara Jacobite Syrian Church](/wiki/Jacobite_Syrian_Christian_Church "Jacobite Syrian Christian Church"), the [Malankara Orthodox Syrian Church](/wiki/Malankara_Orthodox_Syrian_Church "Malankara Orthodox Syrian Church"), the [Mar Thoma Syrian Church](/wiki/Mar_Thoma_Syrian_Church "Mar Thoma Syrian Church"), the [Syro Malankara Church](/wiki/Syro-Malankara_Catholic_Church "Syro-Malankara Catholic Church") and the [Thozhiyur Church](/wiki/Malabar_Independent_Syrian_Church "Malabar Independent Syrian Church").

### Eastern Catholic Churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Christianity&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Eastern Catholic Churches")]

Main article: [Eastern Catholic Churches](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Kanjirappally_Bishop_Mar_Mathew_Arackal_at_Tomb_of_Mar_Varghese_Payyappilly_Palakkappilly.jpg/250px-Kanjirappally_Bishop_Mar_Mathew_Arackal_at_Tomb_of_Mar_Varghese_Payyappilly_Palakkappilly.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kanjirappally_Bishop_Mar_Mathew_Arackal_at_Tomb_of_Mar_Varghese_Payyappilly_Palakkappilly.jpg)

An Eastern Catholic bishop of the [Syro-Malabar](/wiki/Syro-Malabar "Syro-Malabar") Church holding the Mar Thoma Cross which symbolizes the heritage and identity of the [Saint Thomas Christians](/wiki/Saint_Thomas_Christians "Saint Thomas Christians") of [India](/wiki/Christianity_in_India "Christianity in India")

The twenty-three Eastern Catholic Churches are in communion with the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") at the Vatican whilst being rooted in the theological and liturgical traditions of Eastern Christianity. Most of these churches were originally part of the Orthodox East, but have since been reconciled to the Latin Church.

Many of these churches were originally part of one of the above families and so are closely related to them by way of ethos and [liturgical practice](/wiki/Liturgy "Liturgy"). As in the other Eastern churches, married men may become priests, and parish priests administer the [mystery](/wiki/Sacrament "Sacrament") of [confirmation](/wiki/Confirmation "Confirmation") to newborn infants immediately after baptism, via the rite of chrismation; the infants are then administered [Holy Communion](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist").

The Syro-Malabar Church, which is part of the Saint Thomas Christian community in India, follows East Syriac traditions and liturgy. Other Saint Thomas Christians of India, who were originally of the same East Syriac tradition, passed instead to the West Syriac tradition and now form part of Oriental Orthodoxy (some from the Oriental Orthodox in India united with the Catholic Church in 1930 and became the [Syro-Malankara Catholic Church](/wiki/Syro-Malankara_Catholic_Church "Syro-Malankara Catholic Church")). The [Maronite Church](/wiki/Maronite_Church "Maronite Church") claims never to have been separated from Rome, and has no counterpart Orthodox Church out of communion with the Pope. It is therefore inaccurate to refer to it as a "Uniate" Church. The [Italo-Albanian Catholic Church](/wiki/Italo-Albanian_Catholic_Church "Italo-Albanian Catholic Church") has also never been out of communion with Rome, but, unlike the Maronite Church, it resembles the [liturgical rite](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite "Byzantine Rite") of the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church").

### Dissenting movements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Christianity&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Dissenting movements")]

In addition to these four mainstream branches, there are a number of much smaller groups which originated from disputes with the dominant tradition of their original areas. Most of these are either part of the more traditional [Old Believer](/wiki/Old_Believer "Old Believer") movement, which arose from a [schism](/wiki/Schism_(religion) "Schism (religion)") within Russian Orthodoxy, or the more radical [Spiritual Christianity](/wiki/Spiritual_Christianity "Spiritual Christianity") movement. The latter includes a number of diverse "[low-church](/wiki/Low_church "Low church")" groups, from the Bible-centered [Molokans](/wiki/Molokan "Molokan") to the anarchic [Doukhobors](/wiki/Doukhobor "Doukhobor") to the self-mutilating [Skoptsy](/wiki/Skoptsy "Skoptsy"). None of these groups are in communion with the mainstream churches listed above. There are also national dissidents, where ethnic groups want their own nation-church, such as the [Montenegrin Orthodox Church](/wiki/Montenegrin_Orthodox_Church "Montenegrin Orthodox Church") in domicile of the [Serbian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Serbian_Orthodox_Church "Serbian Orthodox Church"). There are also some [Reformed](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") Churches which share characteristics of Eastern Christianity, to varying extents.

#### "True Orthodox" churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Christianity&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: \"True Orthodox\" churches")]

Main article: [True Orthodoxy](/wiki/True_Orthodoxy "True Orthodoxy")

Starting in the 1920s, [parallel hierarchies](/wiki/Greek_Old_Calendarists "Greek Old Calendarists") formed in opposition to local Orthodox churches over ecumenism and other matters. These jurisdictions sometimes refer to themselves as being "True Orthodox". In Russia, underground churches formed and maintained solidarity with the Russian Orthodox Church Outside Russia until the late 1970s. There are now traditionalist Orthodox in every area, though in Asia and Egypt their presence is negligible.

#### Eastern Protestant Churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Christianity&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Eastern Protestant Churches")]

Main article: [Eastern Protestantism](/wiki/Eastern_Protestantism "Eastern Protestantism")

[Eastern Protestant Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Protestant_Christianity "Eastern Protestant Christianity") comprises a collection of heterogeneous Protestant denominations which are mostly the result of [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") Churches adopting Reformation variants of [Orthodox Christian](/wiki/Orthodoxy#Christianity "Orthodoxy") liturgy and worship.[[14]](#cite_note-16)[[15]](#cite_note-17) Some others are the result of [reformations](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation") of [Orthodox Christian](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") beliefs and practices, inspired by the teachings of Western Protestant missionaries.[[16]](#cite_note-18) Denominations of this category include the [Malankara Mar Thoma Syrian Church](/wiki/Malankara_Mar_Thoma_Syrian_Church "Malankara Mar Thoma Syrian Church") ,
[Ukrainian Lutheran Church](/wiki/Ukrainian_Lutheran_Church "Ukrainian Lutheran Church"), [St. Thomas Evangelical Church of India](/wiki/St._Thomas_Evangelical_Church_of_India "St. Thomas Evangelical Church of India"), [Evangelical Orthodox Church](/wiki/Evangelical_Orthodox_Church "Evangelical Orthodox Church"), etc.

##### Byzantine Rite Lutheranism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Christianity&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Byzantine Rite Lutheranism")]

Main article: [Byzantine Rite Lutheranism](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite_Lutheranism "Byzantine Rite Lutheranism")

[Byzantine Rite Lutheranism](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite_Lutheranism "Byzantine Rite Lutheranism") arose in the Ukrainian Lutheran Church around 1926.[[2]](#cite_note-HämmerliMayer2016-2) It sprung up in the region of Galicia and its rites are based on the [Liturgy of Saint John Chrysostom](/wiki/Liturgy_of_Saint_John_Chrysostom "Liturgy of Saint John Chrysostom").[[10]](#cite_note-Bebis2013-11)[[11]](#cite_note-Webber1992-12) The church suffered [persecution under the Communist régime](/wiki/Persecution_of_Christians_in_the_Eastern_Bloc "Persecution of Christians in the Eastern Bloc"), which implemented a policy of [state atheism](/wiki/State_atheism "State atheism").[[17]](#cite_note-Dushnyck1991-19)

## Catholic–Orthodox ecumenism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Christianity&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Catholic–Orthodox ecumenism")]

Ecumenical dialogue since the 1964 meeting between [Pope Paul VI](/wiki/Pope_Paul_VI "Pope Paul VI") and Orthodox Patriarch [Athenagoras I](/wiki/Athenagoras_I "Athenagoras I") has awoken the nearly 1,000-year hopes for Christian unity. Since the lifting of excommunications during the Paul VI and Athenagoras I meeting in Jerusalem there have been other significant meetings between Popes and Ecumenical Patriarchs of Constantinople. One of the most recent meetings was between Benedict XVI and Bartholomew I, who jointly signed the *Common Declaration*. It states that "We give thanks to the Author of all that is good, who allows us once again, in prayer and in dialogue, to express the joy we feel as brothers and to renew *our commitment to move towards **full communion***".[[18]](#cite_note-20)

In 2013 Patriarch [Bartholomew I](/wiki/Bartholomew_I "Bartholomew I") attended the installation ceremony of the new Catholic Pope, [Francis](/wiki/Pope_Francis "Pope Francis"), which was the first time any Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople had ever attended such an installation.[[19]](#cite_note-21)

In 2019, Primate of the [OCU](/wiki/Orthodox_Church_of_Ukraine "Orthodox Church of Ukraine") [Metropolitan of Kyiv and All Ukraine](/wiki/Metropolitan_of_Kyiv_and_all_Ukraine "Metropolitan of Kyiv and all Ukraine") [Epiphanius](/wiki/Epiphanius_I_of_Ukraine "Epiphanius I of Ukraine") stated that "theoretically" the [Orthodox Church of Ukraine](/wiki/Orthodox_Church_of_Ukraine "Orthodox Church of Ukraine") and the [Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church](/wiki/Ukrainian_Greek_Catholic_Church "Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church") could in the future unite into a united church around the Kyiv throne.[[20]](#cite_note-22) In 2019, the primate of the UGCC, [Major Archbishop of Kyiv-Galicia](/wiki/Lists_of_leaders_of_the_Ukrainian_Greek_Catholic_Church "Lists of leaders of the Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church") [Sviatoslav](/wiki/Sviatoslav_Shevchuk "Sviatoslav Shevchuk"), stated that every effort should be made to restore the original unity of the Kyivan Church in its Orthodox and Catholic branches, saying that the restoration of Eucharistic communion between [Rome](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") and [Constantinople](/wiki/Ecumenical_Patriarchate_of_Constantinople "Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople") is not a utopia.[[21]](#cite_note-23)

### Rejection of Uniatism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Christianity&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Rejection of Uniatism")]

At a meeting in [Balamand](/wiki/Balamand "Balamand"), [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon"), in June 1993, the Joint International Commission for the Theological Dialogue between the Catholic Church and the Orthodox Church declared that these initiatives that "led to the union of certain communities with the See of Rome and brought with them, as a consequence, the breaking of communion with their Mother Churches of the East … took place not without the interference of extra-ecclesial interests";[[22]](#cite_note-24) and that what has been called "[uniatism](/wiki/Uniatism "Uniatism")" "can no longer be accepted either as a method to be followed nor as a model of the unity our Churches are seeking" (section 12).

At the same time, the Commission stated:

* 3) Concerning the Eastern Catholic Churches, it is clear that they, as part of the Catholic Communion, have the right to exist and to act in response to the spiritual needs of their faithful.
* 16) The Oriental Catholic Churches who have desired to re-establish full communion with the See of Rome and have remained faithful to it, have the rights and obligations which are connected with this communion.
* 22) Pastoral activity in the Catholic Church, Latin as well as Oriental, no longer aims at having the faithful of one Church pass over to the other; that is to say, it no longer aims at proselytizing among the Orthodox. It aims at answering the spiritual needs of its own faithful and it has no desire for expansion at the expense of the Orthodox Church. Within these perspectives, so that there will be no longer place for mistrust and suspicion, it is necessary that there be reciprocal exchanges of information about various pastoral projects and that thus cooperation between bishops and all those with responsibilities in our Churches, can be set in motion and develop.

## Migration trends

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Christianity&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Migration trends")]

There has been a significant Christian migration in the 20th century from the Near East. Fifteen hundred years ago Christians were the majority population in today's Turkey, Iraq, Syria, Lebanon, Jordan, Palestine and Egypt. In 1914 Christians constituted 25% of the population of the Ottoman Empire. At the beginning of the 21st century Christians constituted 6–7% of the region's population: less than 1% in Turkey, 3% in Iraq, 12% in Syria, 39% in Lebanon, 6% in Jordan, 2.5% in Israel/Palestine and 15–20% in Egypt.

As of 2011 Eastern Orthodox Christians are [among the wealthiest](/wiki/American_upper_class "American upper class") Christians in the United States.[[23]](#cite_note-25) They also tend to be better educated than most other religious groups in America, having a high number of [graduate](/wiki/Academic_degree "Academic degree") (68%) and [post-graduate](/wiki/Post-graduate "Post-graduate") (28%) degrees per capita.[[24]](#cite_note-26)

Scholars and intellectuals agree [Christians have made significant contributions](/wiki/Christian_influences_on_the_Islamic_world "Christian influences on the Islamic world") to Arab and Islamic civilization since the introduction of [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"),[[25]](#cite_note-Hill,_Donald_1993-27)[[26]](#cite_note-28) and they have had a significant impact contributing the culture of the [Middle East and North Africa](/wiki/Middle_East_and_North_Africa "Middle East and North Africa") and other areas.[[27]](#cite_note-29)[[28]](#cite_note-30)[[29]](#cite_note-31) [Byzantine science](/wiki/Byzantine_science "Byzantine science") played an important and crucial role in the [transmission of classical knowledge](/wiki/Greek_contributions_to_the_Islamic_world "Greek contributions to the Islamic world") to the [Islamic world](/wiki/Muslim_world "Muslim world").[[30]](#cite_note-32)

Christians, especially Nestorians, contributed to the Arab Islamic Civilization during the [Umayyads](/wiki/Umayyad_Caliphate "Umayyad Caliphate") and the [Abbasids](/wiki/Abbasids "Abbasids") by translating works of [Greek philosophers](/wiki/Greek_philosophers "Greek philosophers") to [Syriac](/wiki/Syriac_language "Syriac language") and afterwards to [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language").[[25]](#cite_note-Hill,_Donald_1993-27) They also excelled in philosophy, science (such as [Hunayn ibn Ishaq](/wiki/Hunayn_ibn_Ishaq "Hunayn ibn Ishaq"), [Qusta ibn Luqa](/wiki/Qusta_ibn_Luqa "Qusta ibn Luqa"), [Masawaiyh](/wiki/Masawaiyh "Masawaiyh"), [Patriarch Eutychius](/wiki/Patriarch_Eutychius_of_Alexandria "Patriarch Eutychius of Alexandria"), [Jabril ibn Bukhtishu](/wiki/Jabril_ibn_Bukhtishu "Jabril ibn Bukhtishu") etc.) and [theology](/wiki/Theology "Theology") (such as [Tatian](/wiki/Tatian "Tatian"), [Bar Daisan](/wiki/Bar_Daisan "Bar Daisan"), [Babai the Great](/wiki/Babai_the_Great "Babai the Great"), [Nestorius](/wiki/Nestorius "Nestorius"), [Toma bar Yacoub](/wiki/Toma_bar_Yacoub "Toma bar Yacoub"), etc.) and the personal physicians of the Abbasid Caliphs were often [Assyrian](/wiki/Assyrian_people "Assyrian people") Christians such as the long serving [Bukhtishus](/wiki/Bukhtishu "Bukhtishu").[[31]](#cite_note-33)[[32]](#cite_note-34) Many scholars of the [House of Wisdom](/wiki/House_of_Wisdom "House of Wisdom") were of Christian background.[[33]](#cite_note-35)

A hospital and medical training center existed at [Gundeshapur](/wiki/Gundeshapur "Gundeshapur"). The city of Gundeshapur was founded in AD 271 by the Sassanid king [Shapur I](/wiki/Shapur_I "Shapur I"). It was one of the major cities in [Khuzestan](/wiki/Khuzestan "Khuzestan") province of the Persian empire in what is today Iran. A large percentage of the population was Syriacs, most of whom were Christians. Under the rule of [Khusraw I](/wiki/Khusraw_I "Khusraw I"), refuge was granted to Greek [Nestorian Christian](/wiki/Nestorian_Christian "Nestorian Christian") philosophers including the scholars of the Persian School of [Edessa](/wiki/Edessa,_Mesopotamia "Edessa, Mesopotamia") ([Urfa](/wiki/Urfa "Urfa")), also called the academy of [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens"), a Christian theological and medical university. These scholars made their way to Gundeshapur in 529 following the closing of the academy by Emperor Justinian. They were engaged in medical sciences and initiated the first translation projects of medical texts.[[34]](#cite_note-36) The arrival of these medical practitioners from Edessa marks the beginning of the hospital and medical center at Gundeshapur.[[35]](#cite_note-37) It included a medical school and hospital (bimaristan), a pharmacology laboratory, a translation house, a library and an observatory.[[36]](#cite_note-38) Indian doctors also contributed to the school at Gundeshapur, most notably the medical researcher Mankah. Later after Islamic invasion, the writings of Mankah and of the Indian doctor Sustura were translated into Arabic at [Baghdad](/wiki/Baghdad "Baghdad").[[37]](#cite_note-39) [Daud al-Antaki](/wiki/Daud_al-Antaki "Daud al-Antaki") was one of the last generation of influential Arab Christian writers.

[Arab Christians](/wiki/Arab_Christians "Arab Christians") and Arabic-speaking Christians, played important roles in the [Nahda](/wiki/Nahda "Nahda"), and because Arab Christians formed the educated [upper](/wiki/Upper_class "Upper class") and bourgeois classes, they have had a significant impact in politics, business and culture, and most important figures of the Nahda movement were Christian Arabs.[[38]](#cite_note-40) Today [Arab Christians](/wiki/Arab_Christians "Arab Christians") still play important roles in the Arab world, and Christians are relatively wealthy, well educated, and politically moderate.[[39]](#cite_note-41)
